content:
1. Introduction

In the global efforts to address climate change and mitigate its consequences, hydrogen (H2) holds the potential to play a major role in the transition to a sustainable energy economy, as it can act as a clean and renewable energy carrier [1]. However, there are several obstacles that currently hold back its large-scale implementation, especially regarding storage and transportation [2,3]. Under ambient conditions, hydrogen has a low volumetric density (0.081 g H2/L at 1 bar, RT) and is thus stored either as compressed gas at high pressures (24.5 g H2/L at 350 bar, RT) or as liquid at low temperatures (70.8 g H2/L at 1 bar, -253°C) [4]. In its molecular form, hydrogen is highly flammable, posing a significant explosion hazard and substantial safety risks. Additionally, hydrogen transport requires a specialized infrastructure that is not yet widely available, a necessity driven by its hazardous nature and the risk of hydrogen embrittlement in common materials [5,6]. These drawbacks motivate the exploration of alternative hydrogen storage strategies that combine safety, high energy density and ease of handling [4,7].

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
One promising approach that is safe and compatible with existing infrastructure is the use of liquid chemical hydrogen carriers, in which H2 is stored in a chemically bonded form and can be released through catalytic dehydrogenation with solid-phase catalysts [8,9]. Among the many liquid hydrogen storage candidates, ammonia borane (AB, NH3BH3) has become a widely studied system [10–13]. AB offers one of the highest known hydrogen contents (19.6 wt.% H2, 146 g H2/L in the solid state), releases up to three equivalents of hydrogen per mole of AB, is non-toxic, and is stable in highly concentrated aqueous solution, which is the preferred medium for practical release systems.

In principle, AB can release hydrogen via different routes (e.g., thermal decomposition in the solid state or catalytic reactions in solution) [14]. In the present work, AB was used in aqueous phase, where hydrogen is generated by catalytic hydrolysis according to: NH3BH3 + 2H2O → NH4+ + BO2- + 3H2(g), resulting in a gas-releasing reaction environment in which gas-liquid-solid transport can become rate-relevant depending on the reactor concept and catalyst format [15,16].

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
Research on heterogeneous catalysts for the hydrolysis of AB has primarily focused on noble metals, which are expensive and scarce [14–17]. Therefore, the use of abundant and cost-effective non-noble metals represents a more economically viable alternative for hydrogen release. While non-noble metals typically exhibit lower hydrolysis activity than their noble metal counterparts, several studies have shown that their catalytic activity can be drastically enhanced when combined with certain support materials that alter the microenvironment (e.g. on carbon support or with charged polymers [18–20]).

However, despite considerable progress in developing novel, high-performing catalysts, most rely on batch systems with dispersed nanoparticles. These powder-in-liquid approaches complicate catalyst recovery and reuse because the catalyst must be separated from borate-containing reaction mixtures (e.g., by filtration/centrifugation), which is labour-intensive and prone to catalyst loss and contamination. In addition, translating such slurry concepts to continuous operation is non-trivial, as maintaining a stable suspension while avoiding particle sedimentation, agglomeration, clogging, and uncontrolled gas hold-up requires substantial downstream processing and reactor engineering beyond catalyst development [21–26].

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
In addition to catalyst separation and recyclability, sustained operation must accommodate continuous liquid supply to the catalyst surface while hydrogen gas is simultaneously generated and removed. Bubble formation, coalescence, and retention can reduce effective liquid-catalyst contact and introduce additional resistances, particularly if gas hold-up partially blocks transport pathways or reduces the fraction of wetted, catalytically active interface in porous or structured catalysts. Consequently, changes in apparent kinetics and unstable operating regimes may emerge when translating from well-mixed batch screening to continuous-flow concepts unless the reactor and catalyst architecture are designed to manage three-phase transport under gas evolution. These limitations drastically hinder the development of practical hydrogen on demand (HOD) release systems for large-scale implementation. Herein, we aim to close this gap by exploring porous catalytic polymer-based multi-composite membranes.

Porous metal-decorated polymer membranes are very promising for overcoming the limitations of traditional powder catalysts, as they enable continuous catalytic applications under flow conditions, particularly in aqueous media. Many techniques exist to incorporate catalytic functionality into a membrane [27,28].

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content: One straightforward approach is to blend catalyst particles with the polymer-based casting solution, followed by membrane preparation via film casting and phase separation. Additionally, secondary support particles or charged polymers can be added to the casting solution. These additives are then incorporated into the polymer matrix during phase separation and can be used to tune the catalytic microenvironment inside the membrane [29,30]. This fabrication approach also enables the formation of a wide range of pore morphologies [31–35]. Our previous work suggested that the catalytic AB hydrolysis efficiency within porous catalytic materials is significantly influenced by their gas-liquid transport properties, impacting H2 gas release and aqueous liquid hydrogen carrier supply [30]. In the investigation of state-of-the-art catalysts in the literature, this aspect is often overlooked, although a significant number of advanced catalysts are composite materials made from a metal and a highly porous support structure [36–41]. Therefore, the adjustable pore structure of catalytic polymer membranes offers a way to tailor the key performance parameter of mass transport, which is governed by the interplay between surface wettability and pore geometry.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
A gas/liquid membrane contactor provides a large contact area between the two phases and thereby enables an intensification of "conventional" thermal separation processes such as absorption or stripping of gases in/from liquids [42]. Of particular current interest are polymeric hollow-fiber membrane contactors for CO2 absorption [43]. Catalytic membrane contactors, where the membrane additionally serves as solid support of a catalyst, extend the scope of integrated membrane-enabled systems, for example for oxygenation or hydrogenation reactions [44]. Industrial scalability of membrane contactor processes has already been demonstrated [43,45].

Here, we postulate that polymer-based multi-composite membranes are optimal candidates for realizing an effective HOD system, as they allow for the tailoring of both microenvironment and pore structure to optimize their catalytic activity and mass transport properties [29]. Therefore, this study focuses on developing optimized porous nickel-containing membranes for catalytic AB hydrolysis, with the ultimate goal of establishing a novel liquid/gas membrane contactor that combines effective catalytic hydrogen generation with spatial separation of the liquid storage medium and the released gas in a practical HOD system.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
To gain a fundamental understanding of the coupled transport phenomena within this HOD system, we prepared catalytic membranes with systematically varied pore morphology and hydrophobicity. A schematic of the membrane fabrication and the cross-flow membrane contactor investigated in this study is shown in Fig. 1. We investigated how these parameters affect the transport behaviour inside the membranes and the activity of the integrated nickel catalyst. The resulting insights were used to identify key transport limitations and establish structure-performance relationships. Finally, we demonstrate the potential of these membranes for realizing a membrane contactor-based continuous HOD system.

2. Materials and methods

2.1. Materials

Polyethersulfone (PES) Ultrason 6020P was obtained from BASF, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) was from Merck KGaA, and nickel nanopowder (NiNP) with an average particle size of <100 nm, as well as sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were from Sigma-Aldrich. Fumion FAA-3 (10 wt% solution in NMP) and carbon black Vulcan XC 72R, were purchased from Fuel Cell Store. Ammonia borane (AB, NH3BH3) was obtained from TCI.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
2.2. Membrane fabrication

Casting solutions were prepared by dissolving 15 wt.% PES in NMP, followed by the addition of nickel nanoparticles and additives as listed in Table 1. The weight fractions of all additives were calculated in relation to the initial PES solution. Then, the casting solutions were high-shear mixed (3000 rpm) with a vortex mixer (neoLab-72020) for 30 min. Afterward, the casting solutions were degassed for 30 min at 50 mbar at room temperature. Non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS) was employed to prepare porous membranes. For this, the casting solutions were blade-casted with a thickness of 200 μm on a glass plate at a relative humidity of <30% at room temperature. Directly after casting, the obtained film was placed in a precipitation bath of deionized (DI) water for 10 min. The final membranes were then stored in fresh DI water.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
2.3. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX)

A piece of each membrane was first dried from water, then frozen in liquid nitrogen and finally broken to access the cross-section. Before analysis, samples were sputter-coated with PdAu. The SEM images were taken and EDX analyses were performed on a Aureo S LoVac system from Thermo Fisher Scientific at 10-3 Pa, a current of 13 pA and an accelerating voltage of 10 kV (SEM) or 20 kV (EDX).

2.4. Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS)

Three 16 mm diameter samples from three membrane sheets of each composition (n=9) were first dried, then weighed, and finally degraded in 5 mL of aqueous 2 M HNO3 for 24 h to dissolve the incorporated nickel. The amount of leached nickel was then determined via AAS by using an AA spectrometer M Series FS95 from Thermo Scientific.

2.5. Membrane porosity

Three 16 mm diameter samples of each membrane type were weighed in their water-wet state, and their thickness was measured. Afterward, the samples were dried, and their weight, thickness, and diameter were measured again. Then, the membrane porosity was determined using equation (1): ε = Vv/Vt,wet = (mw-md)/(ρwater Vt,wet) * 100%.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
2.6. Contact angle and water or air uptake

All measurements were performed via optical drop shape analysis using the OCA15 system from Dataphysics. Membrane samples for contact angle measurement and water uptake study were prepared by drying from DI water. For performing a static water contact angle determination via the sessile drop method, three pieces of each membrane type were fixed on a glass slide to achieve an even surface. The contact angle was then determined for each membrane piece at three positions (n=9) by dispensing 5 μL of pure water onto the surface, recording the image after 5 s and calculating the contact angle from the drop contour via LaPlace fitting with the corresponding software SCA20. For three membrane samples of each composition (n=3), water uptake was measured by tracing the drop contour.

For the dynamic gas uptake via the captive bubble method, two membrane samples of each composition in different orientations (n=2) were glued onto an acrylic block with a 10 mm drilled hole, so that it is completely sealed with the membrane sample and an unhindered path through the porous membrane was ensured. The sealed side was submerged in ultrapure water, and an air bubble was dispensed below the membrane's surface. By setting the needle thickness as a reference, the bubble volume and thus also the decrease in volume over time could be calculated via the change in bubble contour by the SCA20 software.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
2.7. Immersed catalyst batch experiments

16 mm diameter samples of each membrane were activated overnight in aqueous 100 mM NaBH4 and then washed twice with ultrapure water. Before the reaction, 10 mL of 33 mM AB were placed in a round-bottom flask, heated to 50°C and flushed with argon for 10 min. Then, the respective membrane sample was immersed into the solution, and the batch reaction was conducted for 2 h at 50°C under vigorous stirring with a magnetic stirring bar. The hydrogen formation was tracked through a water displacement setup.

2.8. Batch experiments with directional access to the catalyst

16 mm diameter samples of each membrane were activated with aqueous 33 mM AB overnight, sealed on one surface, and then fixed on the plunger of a syringe, with the open pores facing upwards. The directional catalytic batch reaction was started by filling the syringe with 10 mL of 33 mM AB solution and was conducted for 60 min at room temperature without any form of mixing or shaking. The volume of formed H2 gas was determined in 2-min intervals by water displacement through a connected gas burette. For each membrane sample, the reaction was conducted three times consecutively, with the reaction solution replaced after each 60-min measurement interval. The respective initial rates were calculated from the cumulative H2 volume from an initial 10-min interval using equation (2).

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
2.9. Continuous catalytic cross-flow experiments (liquid/gas membrane contactor)

Membrane samples with a diameter of 25 mm were activated overnight with aqueous 100 mM NaBH4, washed in ultrapure water and then dried in an oven at 50°C in an argon atmosphere. They were put into a cross-flow filtration setup, which was connected to a reservoir with 30 mL of aqueous 33 mM AB solution. The membrane was contacted with the AB solution in cross-flow on the feed side. The flow was set to 0.5 mL/min with a peristaltic pump. On the permeate side, the system was connected to a water displacement setup to record the released cumulative hydrogen gas volume.

2.10. Error analysis and reproducibility

Unless stated otherwise, all data is calculated as mean value and error bars represent the standard deviation (SD) of the respective measurement series. The number of replicates (n) is stated in the respective sections. For time-resolved catalytic experiments, explicitly the immersed batch and cross-flow contactor experiments, the three-phase system was found to be sensitive to initial wetting and transient gas hold-up. Therefore, individual time-series curves are shown as representative examples, while reproducibility of composition-dependent trends was verified across repeated membrane pieces and runs, and additional replicate datasets are provided in the Supporting Information (Figures SI 1 to SI 3).

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
3. Results and discussion

3.1. Porous catalytic membrane fabrication

The membrane variants investigated in this study were based on a polyethersulfone (PES) matrix containing nickel nanoparticles as the non-noble catalyst for ammonia borane hydrolysis. Three compositions were compared to systematically vary microenvironment and transport-relevant properties: an additive-free reference (Ni15), a formulation containing 2 wt.% of the cationic ionomer Fumion (Ni15F2), and a formulation containing 8 wt.% carbon black (Ni15C8).

Fumion was added for two reasons: First, to introduce positive charges into the membrane, thereby tailoring the catalytic microenvironment of the integrated nickel particles, and second, to increase the membrane's surface hydrophobicity due to its aromatic backbone. Hydrophobicity impacts the capillary forces within the membrane's pore network, which governs gas-liquid transport around the catalyst sites during AB hydrolysis. The carbon black was also intended to increase the membrane's hydrophobicity. Additionally, a high content of carbon particles drastically increases the casting solution's viscosity, and the particles can intercalate with the polymer matrix during phase separation. Both effects were expected to significantly alter the membrane's overall pore morphology, which is another key factor that governs capillary forces and consequently mass transport.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
For all three fabricated membranes, we first analysed several basic membrane properties as stated in Table 2. For Ni15, we observed a low Ni utilization of 55%, indicating that a significant portion of the nanoparticles was lost during the NIPS process. However, the addition of Fumion (Ni15F2) and carbon particles (Ni15C8) to the casting solution significantly enhanced uptake. Ni15F2 achieved 88% utilization, while Ni15C8 demonstrated near-quantitative material efficiency. This increase is consistent with a dispersing effect of Fumion or carbon, which may reduce nickel particle agglomeration in the casting solution and facilitates their incorporation into the membrane during preparation.

Table 2 also shows that Ni15C8 exhibits the lowest membrane thickness. This can be attributed to the much higher viscosity of the highly loaded casting solution, which reduces the solvent-nonsolvent exchange rate during phase separation, and thus delayed demixing promotes the formation of a denser, more compact membrane [46,47]. Furthermore, a significant increase in surface water contact angle was observed for membranes containing Fumion (+33° top, +29° bottom) and carbon (+26° top, +17° bottom) compared to Ni15. This suggests the successful incorporation of these hydrophobic additives into the PES matrix and their presence at the membrane surface.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content: Fig. 2 presents cross-sectional SEM images of all three membranes. Each exhibits an anisotropic structure characterized by a pore size gradient, transitioning from smaller pores in the upper region to larger macrovoids at the bottom. The denser upper layer corresponds to the interface that during NIPS initially contacted the water bath. This morphology is attributed to rapid solidification, which limits the time available for coagulation and inhibits the formation of larger pores [31,48]. Water uptake measurements indicate that all three compositions possess high porosity, ranging from 75% to 85%. Despite exhibiting similar cross-sectional morphologies in SEM images, Ni15F2 has a slightly higher porosity than Ni15. This aligns with previous observations suggesting improved Ni particle dispersion and consequently reduced pore blocking in Ni15F2 compared to Ni15. In contrast, the Ni15C8 membrane displays a distinct cross-sectional morphology and a correspondingly lower porosity. Although it retains an anisotropic structure, the overall matrix is significantly denser with rougher inner pore surfaces (cf. Fig. 2).

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
Fig. 3 displays SEM images of the top and bottom surfaces of the three membranes. The most prominent observation is the significant difference in pore size between the upper and lower surfaces, a result of the asymmetric formation mechanism discussed previously. While the upper surfaces of all membranes exhibit no recognizable difference in pore size, the lower surfaces differ notably. Specifically, the pore openings of the Ni15F2 membrane are substantially larger than those of the other two compositions. Additionally, the bottom surface of Ni15C8 exhibits increased roughness, which is again attributed to the high particle loading.

In summary, three porous metal catalyst-loaded materials were successfully fabricated, differing in hydrophobicity, pore size, and pore size gradients. More precisely, Ni15 and Ni15F2 have a similar morphology but differ in hydrophobicity, and Ni15F2 and Ni15C8 have a similar hydrophobicity but different morphologies. This specific set of properties enables a systematic comparison regarding the impact of these parameters.

3.2. Liquid-gas transport properties

We further investigated the liquid-gas transport behaviour inside the pore system of the different membranes, by measuring their uptake rate of a gas (air).

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
To this end, the membranes were first immersed in water to form a liquid meniscus at the pore openings and then brought into contact with an air bubble positioned beneath the membrane surface (Fig. 4). Hereafter we will refer to this uptake rate as the gas-water displacement rate. It should be noted, that fully wetted membranes exhibited no gas uptake in this setup, demonstrating that the buoyancy of the gas bubble was not sufficient to overcome the capillary pressure of water in a fully wetted pore system. Consequently, we utilized dry membrane samples immersed in water for 15 s prior to measurement. Therefore, the gas-water displacement rate should correlate with the dewetting susceptibility of surface pore openings.

All three membranes displayed a lower gas-water displacement rate on the top side, characterised by smaller surface pores (cf. Fig. 3), compared to the bottom side. According to the Young-Laplace equation (3), smaller pores result in higher capillary pressures, making it more difficult for gas to penetrate and dewet the pores, which is consistent with this observation. The explanation based on capillary forces and pore blocking by liquid also matches the fact that Ni15, as the most hydrophilic membrane, exhibits the lowest gas-water displacement rate from both sides. A more hydrophilic pore surface further increases the capillary pressure of water, and thus the force that gas must overcome for entry into pore openings of the same size [49].

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
Notably, although the top-side pore sizes of Ni15F2 and Ni15C8 are similar, and the water contact angle of Ni15F2 is larger than that of Ni15C8, the gas-water displacement rate for Ni15C8 was significantly higher. This may suggest that Ni15C8 possesses a higher effective surface porosity, potentially due to carbon particles disrupting the dense polymer matrix. Alternatively, the presence of carbon particles may increase the roughness of the inner pore surface (cf. Fig. 2). Other studies have already shown that rougher hydrophobic surfaces can promote water dewetting at moderate contact angles [50–52]. Conversely, on the bottom surface, the displacement rate for Ni15F2 was significantly higher than for Ni15C8. This corresponds to the large pore openings observed on the bottom side of Ni15F2 (cf. Fig. 3).

In contrast to the stark differences in gas-water displacement rates, the uptake rates of water from the surface into the underlying pore systems of the membranes, displayed in Fig. 5 and further referred to as water-gas displacement rates, show only minor differences. This similarity may stem from the fact that all three membranes have water contact angles suggesting moderate to nearly complete hydrophobic behaviour, resulting in generally relatively low water wetting tendencies.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
3.3. Catalytic activity in stirred batch experiment

To evaluate the general catalytic behaviour of the materials, we conducted stirred batch experiments in 10 mL of a 33 mM aqueous AB solution at 50°C. The cumulative volume of hydrogen formed was measured using a water displacement setup (Fig. 6), and the time-dependent H2 formation rate r was calculated using Equation (4). The H2 formation rate was normalized to the amount of nickel determined using AAS analysis in order to ensure comparability independently of the nickel content.

If the reaction rate depends solely on the AB concentration, the profile should follow pseudo-first-order kinetics. Consequently, we calculated a theoretical H2 formation profile based on the experimentally determined initial rates of Ni15C8 for comparison (Fig. 6). Absolute hydrogen release rates varied between membrane coupons due to the sensitivity of three-phase wetting and gas hold-up to handling and initial conditions. Therefore, the time-series shown are presented as representative examples, while the composition-dependent trends are supported by repeated measurements and consistent transport characterization.

Examining the data sets for the cumulative H2 volume, Ni15 exhibited the lowest volume and thus the lowest performance after 2 h. In contrast, Ni15F2 and Ni15C8 achieved full conversion, resulting in data that resemble a saturation curve.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
Converting the cumulative volume into an H2 formation rate allows for a clearer visualization of time-dependent catalytic activity. Comparing the measured reaction profile with the theoretical pseudo-first-order kinetics reveals a distinct deviation for all membranes, particularly Ni15. Specifically, the reaction rate drops noticeably after just 10 min. This divergence from theoretical kinetics suggests that a process restricting mass transport develops within the membranes shortly after the reaction begins. Additional post-run AAS results indicate no systematic decrease in Ni content within the measurement variability, suggesting that Ni loss during the batch tests was negligible. Thus, significant loss of nickel from the membrane during the experiment is considered unlikely within the resolution of the applied analysis.

Instead, these observations are likely explained by gas-liquid transport limitations. As discussed previously, the gas-water displacement rate reflects the ability of gas bubbles to overcome the capillary forces of water-wetted pore openings. Although we measured this via gas uptake from outside into the porous membrane, the release of generated H2 bubbles from a wetted pore system requires a comparable dewetting process.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
At the start of the experiment (cf. Fig. 6), dissolved AB diffuses into the water-filled pore system of the membranes and is catalytically hydrolysed to H2 gas, followed by gas release from the membranes. This initial gas release was lowest for Ni15, aligning with the low gas-water displacement rate we observed for this membrane (cf. Fig. 4). A limited gas release further indicates that not all formed H2 bubbles were expelled, with some permanently getting trapped in the pore pathways within the membrane. Over time, this could lead to bubble accumulation and eventually blocking of pore pathways, restricting the diffusion of dissolved AB to nickel sites and resulting in a sharp drop in the catalytic H2 release rate (Fig. 6, schematic drawing). This hypothesis is supported by our previous work, which identified indications of gas-water displacement limitations during AB hydrolysis [30].

Additionally, we observed that the initial rate of Ni15 and Ni15F2 measured with a pristine membrane sample (Fig. 6) was not fully recovered when using the same membrane sample across multiple cycles (cf. Figure SI 1). This suggests that, for both Ni15 and Ni15F2, a portion of the H2 bubbles formed may become persistently trapped within the membrane, possibly at bottlenecks along the pore pathways. However, this was not observed for Ni15C8, indicating that all accumulated bubbles were eventually released by the end of the first batch experiment. This effect may be linked to the high pore surface roughness in this membrane, as the rough hydrophobic surface could promote dewetting of pore pathway bottlenecks inside the membrane by reducing the effective capillary pressure at the gas-water interface.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
3.4. Catalytic activity in directional batch experiments

In the directional batch experiments, we used membrane pieces of defined diameter sealed each on one side and contacted them with 10 mL of a 33 mM aqueous solution of AB at room temperature, to investigate the H2 gas generation under conditions of directional transport of gas and liquid in opposite directions through the same pore pathways (Fig. 7). In this experiment, top refers to the transport of fluids into and out of the membrane through the surface pores of the top side (smaller pores) and bot correspondingly refers to that through those of the bottom side. The sum value represents the sum of the experimentally determined values of top and bot. Free depicts a third reference experiment in which the membranes were not sealed on either side, allowing unhindered transport in all directions (cf. Fig. 6).

All three membranes exhibited orientation-dependent H2 release rate differences. Ni15 and Ni15F2 showed higher release rates in the top orientation, whereas Ni15C8 showed the reverse. Interestingly, this pattern mirrors the water-gas displacement behaviour, as determined by water droplet uptake (cf. Fig. 5), but with an inverse correlation: Higher H2 release rates correspond to slower water-gas displacement.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
Under directional conditions, gas and liquid transport, as well as gas-liquid and liquid-gas displacement, need to occur within the same pore pathway. Consequently, all these processes become critical for the H2 bubble release rate from the membrane. If the membrane properties promote efficient water-gas displacement, H2 bubbles inside the membrane may encounter a stronger counter-force from water, inhibiting their movement toward the surface pore openings. In contrast, when the membrane is fully immersed, as in the stirred batch experiment, an H2 bubble can travel along a pore pathway driven by buoyancy and exit through a surface pore opening, while reaction solution can still simultaneously enter from the opposite side of the membrane. In other words, under directional transport conditions, an additional factor is the counter-force H2 bubbles experience from liquid-gas displacement.

This explanation is supported by the free condition results. The H2 formation rate in the unsealed experiment significantly exceeded the sum of the individual directional rates (top + bottom). This suggests that when the pore system is accessible from all sides, the entry of reaction solution and the release of formed H2 bubbles can occur simultaneously through different pore pathways, thereby significantly enhancing mass transport efficiency.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
3.5. Catalytic liquid/gas membrane contactor in cross-flow

To further investigate direction-dependent transport and evaluate the materials' potential as a HOD system, we conducted cross-flow experiments representing a catalytic liquid-gas membrane contactor. The concept involves feeding the liquid hydrogen carrier along one side of the membrane to contact the immobilized catalyst. We hypothesized that gas bubbles formed within the smaller pores would follow the path of least resistance toward the larger pores. This mechanism would enable simultaneous catalytic conversion and the spatial separation of the liquid carrier from the generated H2 gas.

Dried membranes were mounted in a cross-flow cell, and 30 mL of a 33 mM aqueous AB solution was circulated across the membrane surface at 0.5 mL/min using a peristaltic pump. Cumulative H2 release on the permeate side was quantified using a water displacement setup identical to that used in the directional batch experiments (cf. Fig. 7). When the larger pores were in contact with the AB solution, no H2 release was detected on the permeate side after 30 min for any membrane composition, leading to the termination of the experiment. Conversely, when the smaller pores faced the solution, gas release was observed on the permeate side either immediately or within 5 min. These findings suggest that the asymmetric pore structure exerts a critical influence on transport processes, supporting the hypothesis of directional transport.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
From the measured cumulative H2 release we again calculated the time-dependent rate (Equation (3)), which is illustrated in Fig. 8. After the H2 release starts, the less hydrophilic membranes (Ni15F2 and Ni15C8) exhibited an increasing rate, which can be attributed to the progressive wetting of the internal pore surface. In contrast, Ni15 showed a decreasing initial rate. This is consistent with the stirred batch experiments, where we ascribed a similar decline to the potential blocking of active sites by gas bubbles trapped within the pore system.

Subsequently, Ni15 and Ni15F2 reached a constant rate, suggesting that a steady state in the phase boundary position has been reached. Ni15C8 displayed a continuous increase in rate, which implied a gradual expansion of the wetted surface area and consequently resulted in higher release rates. We hypothesize that liquid penetration continues throughout the experiment, likely facilitated by the membrane's distinct morphology, which features smaller macro-voids and a partial sponge structure (cf. Fig. 2). That again supports the overall improved transport properties, which we discussed for other results earlier.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
When we subsequently performed a washing step by flushing the membrane with ultrapure water for 30 min and introducing fresh AB solution, distinct recovery behaviours were observed. The H2 rates for Ni15 and Ni15F2 resumed immediately at their previous steady-state levels. Conversely, the rate for Ni15C8 reset to a lower initial value before rising again. This suggests that the reaction solution likely penetrates deeper into the cross-section of Ni15C8 and thus, after rinsing, reactants must re-diffuse into the deep pore network to restore the maximum rate. This supports the assumption that a larger portion of the Ni15C8 cross-section is wetted during operation.

During cross-flow operation, liquid breakthrough was observed, i.e., transport of the aqueous reaction solution to the permeate side under gas-evolving conditions. This observation indicates that increasing breakthrough pressure is critical for robust operation. In contrast to classical hydrophobic membrane contactors [42,43], where liquid entry pressure (LEP) describes pressure-driven penetration of a non-wetted pore network, the present catalytic contactor concept is intentionally operated in a wetted state on the feed side and exhibits spontaneous wetting by the aqueous AB solution. The relevant failure mode is therefore governed by the coupled effects of capillary-driven penetration and simultaneous in-pore gas formation.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
3.6. Integrated discussion

In this study, we investigated the influence of pore structure and material properties on gas and liquid transport within catalytically active porous membranes, with the aim of optimizing hydrogen release from liquid chemical hydrogen carriers in a membrane contactor setup. We fabricated and characterized three membrane compositions (Ni15, Ni15F2, and Ni15C8) to correlate their transport properties with catalytic performance. Compared to the Ni15 membrane with no additives other than the nickel catalyst, the addition of carbon black (Ni15C8) and Fumion (Ni15F2) significantly altered membrane morphology, pore size, and hydrophilicity. These differences in structure and wettability were accompanied by changes in gas-liquid interactions and, consequently, the catalytic hydrogen formation rates. Furthermore, Ni15F2 and Ni15C8 exhibited improved nickel incorporation and higher nickel utilization (i.e., lower nickel loss during membrane preparation), attributed to stronger interactions between the catalyst and the polymer matrix.

Across four distinct experimental configurations, we observed divergent behaviours among the three materials, allowing us to draw broader conclusions regarding how hydrophilicity, morphology, and pore size govern multiphase transport in porous matrices.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
In both transport and catalytic experiments, the hydrophilic Ni15 membrane exhibited significant transport limitations. This behavior is consistent with the blocking of active sites by evolving gas bubbles, which progressively impeded the hydrogen release rate. In contrast, the Ni15C8 membrane, characterized by a distinct pore structure and reduced hydrophilicity due to high carbon loading, the highest hydrogen release rates and the most favorable transport behaviour across all setups. We interpret this as consistent with efficient gas bubble release, which minimizes pore blockage and maximizes hydrogen production efficiency. While Ni15F2 also outperformed Ni15 due to enhanced nickel distribution and higher hydrophobicity, it experienced greater mass transport limitations than Ni15C8. This suggests that the superior performance of Ni15C8 stems not only from altered hydrophilicity but fundamentally from its modified matrix structure.

Beyond specific material comparisons, two general mechanisms regarding transport in catalytic membranes can be proposed from these experiments. First, when gas and liquid transport are confined to the same pore channels (e.g., in dead-end pores or sealed configurations), liquid entry may become the rate-limiting step due to the counter-force of gas dewetting and bubble release. Under these conditions, pore structures with higher capillary forces facilitate liquid entry against gas pressure, resulting in higher H2 formation rates and improved catalyst utilization.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
Second, when the pore network is accessible from multiple sides, transport is expected to follow the path of least resistance: liquid enters via smaller pores with high capillary pressure, while gas exits through the large pores with low capillary pressure. This decoupling facilitates directional transport in asymmetric membranes, leading to higher overall conversion. The results of cross-flow experiments are consistent with this hypothesis of directional gas and liquid transport through the asymmetric membrane structures, by the observation that gas release on the permeate side was dependent on the membranes' orientation. This further highlights the importance of pore architecture in optimizing catalytic performance. In addition, the cross-flow experiments served as a proof of concept for the idea of using an asymmetrically porous catalytic membrane as a contactor for an HOD system and we were able to observe how the transport processes in this system also largely depend on the membrane properties.

Overall, this study provides valuable insights into the design of catalytic membranes for hydrogen generation from liquid chemical hydrogen storage systems but also gives more generally applicable insights that can be relevant for the design of similar three-phase-systems e.g., of gas diffusion electrode/catalyst layers in membrane electrode assemblies (MEA) [53–56].

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
4. Conclusion

This study demonstrates that asymmetric porous catalytic membranes can function as liquid/gas contactors for hydrogen release and reveals that pore morphology and surface properties (in particular wetting) are decisive parameters that determine functionality and performance. The superior performance of Ni15C8 in most experiments illustrates how the interplay of hydrophobicity, surface structure and cross-sectional pore morphology enhance the hydrogen release rates by reducing pore blockage by gas bubbles and improving liquid distribution. This effect is particularly evident when compared with reference membranes differing in hydrophobicity and pore morphology. These findings highlight that in a three-phase system where gas evolves from liquid on a porous solids surface, efficient simultaneous transport of gas and liquid is a central parameter for catalytic efficiency, both in general and specifically for our novel catalytic membrane contactor system. In this context, ammonia borane serves as a model system to establish structure-transport-activity relationships that can be broadly relevant for aqueous hydrogen carriers in general.

Despite the promising perspective, the current system is not yet sufficient for practical application. Liquid breakthrough was observed for all tested samples, indicating a lack of long-term stability and scalability due to unavoidable pore wetting, which currently limits the translation beyond proof-of-concept experiments.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution

content:
This observation indicates that increasing breakthrough resistance (i.e., increasing the effective breakthrough pressure) is critical for robust operation of the liquid/gas membrane contactor concept. While the novel membrane contactor concept based on directional transport is validated, further optimisation is required before deployment in real energy infrastructure can be considered.

To overcome the essential issue, strategies to increase liquid breakthrough pressure must be implemented, particularly when considering capillary membrane configurations that need to be operated under higher pressures due to the pressure drop along the capillaries. Incorporating an additional hydrophobic layer while maintaining constant hydrogen release performance and conducting durability tests represent promising approaches to bridge this gap. Establishing our multi-composite membranes as both catalytic and transport-regulating elements provides a framework for further developments in three-phase catalytic processes, with potential applicability extending from hydrogen storage to other technologies with a similar framework, such as, for example, hydrogen production by water electrolysis or carbon dioxide reduction to chemicals.

citation.title: Anisotropic porous polymer-based nickel composites as catalytic membrane contactors for directional hydrogen gas release from ammonia borane solution
